enrichment.short_description: Domain portfolio manager that tracks expiration, DNS, SSL, and security across every registrar in one dashboard.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Domain Locker is a domain portfolio management platform that centralizes monitoring for domains spread across multiple registrars. It tracks expiration dates, DNS records, SSL certificates, WHOIS changes, and uptime, and can alert owners before a domain lapses or its configuration changes unexpectedly.

The project is open source under the MIT license, so it can be self-hosted for free on your own infrastructure. A hosted cloud version is also offered for people who would rather not run their own server, with paid tiers based on how many domains you manage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Domain investors, agencies managing client portfolios, and sysadmins who want a single place to watch expiration dates, SSL status, and DNS changes across many domains rather than checking each registrar separately.

features.title: Automatic asset detection
features.description: Discovers SSL certificates, DNS records, registrars, subdomains, and IPs tied to each domain.

features.title: Expiration and change alerts
features.description: Notifies you before a domain expires or when its configuration changes, via email, webhook, Telegram, or Signal.

features.title: Security audits
features.description: Flags security issues and gives recommendations for each domain in your portfolio.

features.title: Uptime and performance monitoring
features.description: Tracks whether domains are online and responding as expected.

features.title: Financial tracking
features.description: Records registration costs, renewal fees, and estimated valuations for profit/loss tracking.

features.title: API and feed access
features.description: Exposes data via a REST API, Prometheus metrics, iCal, and RSS for integration with other tools.

pros: Open source and free to self-host on your own server
Multi-channel alerting beyond just email
Tracks financial value of a domain portfolio, not just DNS/SSL status
API access for integrating with existing monitoring stacks
Discounted pricing for nonprofits and open-source maintainers
cons: Free cloud tier is capped at 5 domains
Larger portfolios require a paid cloud plan or self-hosting effort
Self-hosting requires setting up and maintaining your own server

faqs.question: Is Domain Locker free?
faqs.answer: The self-hosted, open-source version is free to run on your own infrastructure. The hosted cloud version has a free tier limited to 5 domains, with paid plans for larger portfolios.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Domain Locker?
faqs.answer: Yes, the source code is MIT-licensed and available on GitHub for anyone to deploy and modify.

faqs.question: What happens when a domain is about to expire?
faqs.answer: Domain Locker can send expiration alerts through email, webhook, Telegram, Signal, and other supported channels before renewal deadlines.

faqs.question: Does it work with any domain registrar?
faqs.answer: It's designed to track domains regardless of which registrar they were purchased from, aggregating them into a single dashboard.

use_cases.title: Domain portfolio management
use_cases.description: Investors holding many domains across registrars use it to track expiration and value in one place.

use_cases.title: Agency client domain oversight
use_cases.description: Agencies manage renewal dates and SSL status for multiple client domains from a single dashboard.

use_cases.title: Infrastructure monitoring
use_cases.description: Sysadmins self-host it to monitor DNS and SSL changes across owned domains without relying on a third party.

features: Unlimited domains on your own infrastructure

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Domain Locker is an open-source domain portfolio manager with expiration alerts, DNS/SSL monitoring, and a free self-hosted option.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open-source contest judging system used to run ICPC-style programming competitions with automated grading.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DOMjudge is a free, open-source system for running programming contests, most notably ICPC-style competitions. It automates the judging of submitted code against test cases, provides a jury interface for manual review, and runs a live scoreboard for teams and spectators.

The project has been used in real competitions since 2004, including numerous ICPC regional contests such as NWERC and SWERC, and is also used in university courses and online judging systems. It's released under the GNU General Public License and maintained by a community of contributors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Contest organizers, universities running programming competitions, and instructors who need automated code judging for classroom exercises or coding contests.

features.title: Automated judging
features.description: Compiles and runs submitted code against test cases and grades it automatically.

features.title: Modular language support
features.description: Supports a range of programming languages through a pluggable language configuration.

features.title: Jury interface
features.description: Gives judges a dedicated interface to review submissions, resolve edge cases, and manage the contest.

features.title: Live scoreboard
features.description: Displays team standings to contestants and spectators as the contest progresses.

features.title: Security-focused design
features.description: Built with contest integrity in mind, including sandboxed submission execution.

pros: Battle-tested in ICPC regional and world-level contests since 2004
Completely free and open source under the GPL
Supports multiple programming languages
Active development community and mailing list/Slack
cons: Aimed at technical contest organizers; setup requires server administration skills
Interface is utilitarian rather than consumer-friendly
No official hosted SaaS version — you run your own instance

faqs.question: Is DOMjudge free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free, open-source software released under the GNU General Public License.

faqs.question: What contests use DOMjudge?
faqs.answer: It has been used in numerous ICPC regional contests including NWERC and SWERC, as well as university courses and online programming contests.

faqs.question: Does DOMjudge support multiple programming languages?
faqs.answer: Yes, language support is modular so contest organizers can configure which languages are accepted.

faqs.question: Can I run DOMjudge for a small local contest?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be self-hosted for contests of any size, from classroom exercises to large regional competitions.

use_cases.title: University programming contests
use_cases.description: Universities run local or regional ICPC-style contests using DOMjudge for automated grading and a live scoreboard.

use_cases.title: Classroom coding exercises
use_cases.description: Instructors use it to automatically grade programming assignments in courses.

use_cases.title: Regional and national qualifiers
use_cases.description: Organizers of ICPC regional contests rely on DOMjudge to judge submissions across many teams reliably.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: DOMjudge is a free, open-source automated judging system for ICPC-style programming contests, self-hosted by universities worldwide.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted platform for running experience-sampling and ecological momentary assessment research studies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ESMira is an open-source, decentralized platform for running Experience Sampling Method (ESM), Ambulatory Assessment (AA), and Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) research studies. It's developed by the Department of Psychological Methodology at Karl Landsteiner Privatuniversität in Austria and described in a peer-reviewed paper published in Behavior Research Methods.

Researchers install ESMira on their own PHP-enabled webserver, where they design questionnaires, schedule prompts sent to participants' phones, and collect fully anonymous data without routing it through any third-party server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Academic researchers and study administrators running ESM, EMA, or ambulatory diary-style studies in psychology, medicine, or behavioral science who need participant data to stay under their own control.

features.title: Decentralized self-hosting
features.description: Each study administrator hosts their own instance, so studies and their data operate independently on separate servers.

features.title: Participant anonymity
features.description: Designed so participant data is never sent anywhere beyond the server hosting that specific study.

features.title: PHP-only server requirement
features.description: Runs on any standard webserver with PHP, without complex infrastructure dependencies.

features.title: Automatic self-updates
features.description: Can back itself up, pull the newest version from GitHub, and migrate its data structure automatically.

features.title: Customizable studies
features.description: Lets administrators configure surveys, prompt schedules, and data collection rules for their specific research design.

pros: Participant data never leaves the researcher's own server
Free and open source under AGPL
Simple PHP-only hosting requirement, no exotic infrastructure
Backed by an academic department with a published methodology paper
Built-in self-update mechanism
cons: Requires the researcher to set up and maintain their own server
Documentation and design are oriented toward academic researchers, not general users
Niche, research-specific tool rather than a general-purpose survey platform
Interface is less polished than commercial EMA/EMS tools

faqs.question: What is ESMira used for?
faqs.answer: It's used by researchers to run experience-sampling, ambulatory assessment, and ecological momentary assessment studies, sending scheduled prompts to participants and collecting their responses.

faqs.question: Is ESMira free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the AGPL license.

faqs.question: Do I need to be a developer to set it up?
faqs.answer: You need access to a PHP-enabled webserver, but the project is designed so study administrators without deep technical expertise can install and configure it.

faqs.question: How is participant privacy protected?
faqs.answer: Because each study runs on its own decentralized server, participant data is only ever sent to the server hosting that specific study, not to any central third party.

use_cases.title: Psychology diary studies
use_cases.description: Research teams schedule repeated prompts to participants' phones to collect momentary mood or behavior data.

use_cases.title: Multi-site decentralized studies
use_cases.description: Universities running studies at different sites each host their own independent ESMira instance.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ESMira is a free, open-source, self-hosted platform from Karl Landsteiner Privatuniversitat for running experience-sampling research studies.

enrichment.short_description: Community-run, free and open-source app catalog offering FOSS Android apps outside the Google Play Store.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: F-Droid is an installable catalog of free and open source software (FOSS) applications for Android, functioning as an alternative to the Google Play Store. It has operated since 2010, maintained by community contributors and funded through donations rather than corporate ownership.

Because F-Droid builds many of the apps it distributes itself from public source code, it emphasizes verifiability and freedom from tracking, and organizations can also run their own private F-Droid repository.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Privacy-conscious Android users who want FOSS alternatives to mainstream apps, open-source developers distributing apps outside Google Play, and organizations that want to self-host an internal app repository.

features.title: Curated FOSS app catalog
features.description: Lists free and open source Android apps vetted for inclusion in the official repository.

features.title: Reproducible builds
features.description: Many apps are built directly from source by F-Droid's own infrastructure rather than repackaging developer binaries.

features.title: No default tracking
features.description: The client and cataloged apps are checked for tracking libraries, with anti-features flagged when present.

features.title: Custom repositories
features.description: Anyone can host their own F-Droid-compatible repository to distribute apps privately or to a specific audience.

features.title: In-app update notifications
features.description: Notifies users when a new version of an installed app is available.

pros: Completely free with no ads or paid tiers
Privacy-respecting alternative to the Google Play Store
Open, community-based governance rather than corporate control
Anyone can self-host a custom app repository
cons: Smaller app catalog than Google Play
Some apps lag behind their Play Store releases due to reproducible-build requirements
Android only
Recent changes to Android sideloading policy create uncertainty for F-Droid's distribution model

faqs.question: Is F-Droid free?
faqs.answer: Yes, F-Droid is free and funded entirely by donations.

faqs.question: Is F-Droid safe to use?
faqs.answer: F-Droid vets apps for inclusion and flags known tracking or non-free dependencies as anti-features, and many apps are built by F-Droid's own infrastructure rather than repackaged binaries.

faqs.question: Can I host my own F-Droid repository?
faqs.answer: Yes, anyone can set up a custom repository and point the F-Droid client at it to distribute apps privately.

faqs.question: Does F-Droid require Google Play Services?
faqs.answer: No, F-Droid and the apps it distributes are generally designed to work without Google Play Services.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused app sourcing
use_cases.description: Users install FOSS apps vetted for privacy instead of relying on the Google Play Store.

use_cases.title: Open-source app distribution
use_cases.description: Developers publish their FOSS apps to F-Droid's catalog to reach users who prefer open-source software.

use_cases.title: Private organizational app repository
use_cases.description: Organizations self-host a custom F-Droid repository to distribute internal or approved apps to managed devices.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: F-Droid is a free, open-source, community-run app store for Android offering FOSS apps and the option to self-host your own repository.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source Docker app that resizes, crops, and compresses images on the fly, like a self-hosted Cloudinary.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Flyimg is a Dockerized, open-source image-processing service that resizes, crops, and optimizes images on the fly using ImageMagick, offering a self-hosted alternative to hosted image CDNs like Cloudinary. It fetches a source image, applies the requested transformations via URL parameters, caches the output, and serves optimized files.

The project is released under the AGPL-3.0 license, is available as a single Docker container, and also offers an optional hosted SaaS version at flyimg.io for teams that don't want to run their own infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Developers and teams who want an on-premise, self-hosted alternative to commercial image CDNs for resizing, cropping, and format-optimizing images at request time.

features.title: On-the-fly resize and crop
features.description: Transforms images based on parameters passed in the request URL.

features.title: Modern format output
features.description: Serves AVIF, WebP, MozJPEG, PNG, GIF, and optionally JPEG XL for smaller file sizes.

features.title: Automatic format negotiation
features.description: The o_auto option selects the best output format based on the browser's Accept header.

features.title: Efficient caching
features.description: Stores processed images so repeat requests are served from cache instead of reprocessed.

features.title: Face detection and blurring
features.description: Supports face-aware cropping and blurring as part of the image pipeline.

features.title: Single-container deployment
features.description: Ships as one Docker container running nginx and php-fpm.

pros: Free and open source under AGPL-3.0
Deploys as a single Docker container
Supports modern, bandwidth-saving formats like AVIF and WebP
Active GitHub project with JetBrains open-source sponsorship
Optional hosted SaaS version for teams that don't want to self-host
cons: AGPL license carries copyleft obligations for modified network-hosted deployments
Requires Docker and server management to self-host
Fewer built-in features than full commercial media CDNs

faqs.question: Is Flyimg free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the self-hosted software is free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Can I use Flyimg without self-hosting it?
faqs.answer: Yes, a hosted version is available at flyimg.io for people who don't want to run their own container.

faqs.question: What image formats does Flyimg support?
faqs.answer: It outputs AVIF, WebP, MozJPEG, PNG, GIF, and optionally JPEG XL, and can auto-select the best format for the requesting browser.

faqs.question: How is Flyimg deployed?
faqs.answer: It runs as a single Docker container bundling nginx and php-fpm.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted image CDN
use_cases.description: Teams replace a commercial image CDN with a self-hosted Flyimg container to control costs and data residency.

use_cases.title: CMS thumbnail generation
use_cases.description: Agencies generate on-the-fly thumbnails and responsive image variants for content-managed websites.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Flyimg is a free, open-source Docker app for on-the-fly image resizing and optimization, a self-hosted alternative to Cloudinary.

enrichment.short_description: Gamified habit and to-do tracker that turns real-life tasks into an RPG, free to use with optional paid subscriptions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Habitica turns habit-building and task management into a role-playing game: completing habits, dailies, and to-dos earns experience and gold, while missed dailies cost health. It's fully usable for free, with an optional subscription that unlocks cosmetic rewards and supports the developers rather than gating core functionality.
The underlying codebase, HabitRPG, is open source under the GPL-3.0 license and can be self-hosted, though the primary way most people use Habitica is through the official hosted app at habitica.com.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: People who want extra motivation for habit tracking and task management through gamification, often paired with community 'parties' and guilds for social accountability.

features.title: Habits, dailies, and to-dos
features.description: A task system where completing items earns rewards and missing dailies has in-game consequences.

features.title: Avatar customization
features.description: Gold and gems earned in-app unlock gear, pets, and mounts for a customizable character.

features.title: Parties and guilds
features.description: Social groups where members can hold each other accountable and take on quests together.

features.title: Challenges
features.description: Community or private challenges let groups compete around shared goals.

features.title: Cross-platform apps
features.description: Available as a web app plus native iOS and Android apps.

features.title: Open-source self-hosting
features.description: The GPL-3.0 codebase can be deployed on your own infrastructure by technically inclined users.

pros: Core task-tracking functionality is entirely free
Strong social and gamification mechanics for accountability
Cross-platform: web, iOS, and Android
Open source with a community-supported self-hosting path
Long-running, active community
cons: Gamified approach can feel gimmicky for users who want a plain to-do list
Paid subscription only unlocks cosmetics, not extra productivity features
Official self-hosting support is limited; most self-host setups are community-maintained forks

faqs.question: Is Habitica free?
faqs.answer: Yes, all core task-tracking and gamification features are free. A subscription adds cosmetic items and supports development.

faqs.question: What does a Habitica subscription include?
faqs.answer: Subscribers get exclusive monthly items and a special pet, but the subscription does not unlock any productivity feature withheld from free users.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Habitica?
faqs.answer: The codebase is open source under GPL-3.0, and community projects exist for running your own instance, though it isn't officially packaged by the core team.

faqs.question: Is there a group or family plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Habitica offers a group plan priced per member for parties, families, or friend groups wanting shared accountability features.

use_cases.title: Solo habit building
use_cases.description: Individuals use the game mechanics to stay motivated on daily habits and routines.

use_cases.title: Group accountability
use_cases.description: Friends or families join a party or use the group plan to keep each other on track.

features: Full habit, daily, and to-do tracking

pricing_plans.billing_cycle: monthly for first account, plus $3/month per additional member

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Habitica is a free, gamified habit and to-do tracker with optional paid subscriptions, available on web, iOS, and Android.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source platform for tracking and managing a houseplant collection, with reminders and a plant community.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HortusFox is a self-hosted, open-source platform for keeping track of houseplants. It provides a dashboard for plant inventory, care task tracking, calendar and weather integration, and a REST API, released under the MIT license for anyone to deploy on their own server.

It's built for plant enthusiasts who want a private, data-owning alternative to commercial plant-care apps, and it includes collaborative features like group chat and photo sharing for households or plant-sharing communities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Individual plant hobbyists tracking care schedules, and households or plant-collective groups who want to share one instance across multiple people.

features.title: Plant dashboard and inventory
features.description: Central view of your plant collection with details for each plant.

features.title: Task tracking
features.description: Set and track recurring care tasks like watering or fertilizing.

features.title: Calendar and weather integration
features.description: Ties care schedules to a calendar and local weather data.

features.title: Group chat and photo sharing
features.description: Lets multiple users on the same instance communicate and share plant photos.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Available since version 3.1 for integrating HortusFox with other tools.

features.title: Activity history
features.description: Tracks changes and care activity over time per plant and per user.

pros: Free and open source under the MIT license
Self-hosted, so plant and photo data stays private
Actively maintained with a Discord community
REST API for custom integrations
Supports multiple collaborating users on one instance
cons: Requires self-hosting; there's no official managed hosting from the maintainers
No official native mobile app
Smaller community than mainstream consumer plant apps

faqs.question: Is HortusFox free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source under the MIT license.

faqs.question: Can multiple people use the same HortusFox instance?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports multiple user accounts and includes group chat and photo sharing for collaborative plant tracking.

faqs.question: Does HortusFox have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, a REST API has been available since version 3.1.

faqs.question: Is there official hosting available?
faqs.answer: The maintainers point to third-party community hosting options but disclaim responsibility for those external providers; self-hosting is the primary supported path.

use_cases.title: Personal plant care tracking
use_cases.description: A hobbyist logs watering, fertilizing, and repotting schedules for their houseplant collection.

use_cases.title: Shared household plant tracking
use_cases.description: A household or plant-sharing group uses one instance with multiple accounts to coordinate care.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: HortusFox is a free, open-source, self-hosted app for tracking houseplant care with reminders, a calendar, and a REST API.

enrichment.short_description: Free, self-hosted Docker tool for bulk image compression and format conversion across 70+ formats, with offline AI background removal.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ImgCompress is a self-hosted, open-source Docker tool built by Karim Zouine for batch image compression, format conversion, and resizing across more than 70 formats, including HEIC, PSD, and AVIF. It also includes offline AI-based background removal and can assemble multi-page PDFs from a batch of images.
Everything runs locally in the container with no cloud upload, no telemetry, and no license checks — once the Docker image is pulled, it works without an internet connection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: People who need to batch-process large photo libraries or build an in-house image pipeline without sending files to a third-party cloud service.

features.title: 70+ format support
features.description: Handles conversion and compression across formats including HEIC, HEIF, PSD, AVIF, and PDF.

features.title: Local AI background removal
features.description: Removes image backgrounds using a bundled model that runs entirely offline.

features.title: Batch processing
features.description: Compresses entire photo libraries in parallel using multiple CPU cores.

features.title: PDF creation
features.description: Converts a batch of images into a structured, paginated PDF.

features.title: Privacy-first design
features.description: No telemetry, no cloud processing, and no external API calls.

features.title: Web UI and CLI
features.description: Offers both a web interface for everyday use and a CLI for scripted workflows.

pros: Completely free with no subscriptions or license checks
Works fully offline once deployed, protecting data privacy
Handles bulk/batch operations efficiently across many CPU cores
Includes AI background removal without needing a cloud API key
Featured on the Awesome Self-Hosted list
cons: Maintained by a single developer, which carries a bus-factor risk
Requires Docker to run; no official hosted version
Interface is functional rather than polished

faqs.question: Is ImgCompress free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's permanently free with no subscriptions, license checks, or expiration dates.

faqs.question: Does ImgCompress need an internet connection?
faqs.answer: No, after the initial Docker image pull it runs entirely offline.

faqs.question: What image formats does it support?
faqs.answer: Over 70 formats, including HEIC, HEIF, PSD, AVIF, and PDF output.

faqs.question: Can it remove image backgrounds without a cloud AI service?
faqs.answer: Yes, background removal runs locally using a bundled AI model rather than calling an external API.

use_cases.title: Bulk photo library compression
use_cases.description: Photographers compress and convert large photo libraries in bulk without uploading files to the cloud.

use_cases.title: In-house image processing pipeline
use_cases.description: Developers self-host it as a backend for compressing and converting images without relying on cloud image APIs.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: ImgCompress is a free, self-hosted Docker tool for bulk image compression, format conversion, and offline AI background removal.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source personal library app for tracking books read and to-read, a private alternative to Goodreads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Jelu is a self-hosted, open-source personal reading tracker positioned as a private alternative to Goodreads. It automatically fetches book metadata, can import reading history from Goodreads exports or ISBN lists, and lets you organize books into custom shelves with reviews and reading statistics broken down by year and month.

It's deployed via a JAR file or Docker, supports multiple user accounts, and offers an interactive REST API along with LDAP or reverse-proxy authentication for use in shared or organizational settings.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Readers who want to keep their reading data private and self-hosted instead of relying on Goodreads, and households or book clubs sharing one instance across multiple accounts.

features.title: Automatic metadata fetching
features.description: Pulls book details automatically when adding titles to your library.

features.title: Goodreads and ISBN import
features.description: Imports existing reading history from a Goodreads export or a list of ISBNs.

features.title: Custom shelves and tags
features.description: Organizes books into user-defined shelves and tags.

features.title: Reviews and reading history
features.description: Tracks books read by year and month, with space for personal reviews.

features.title: REST API with tokens
features.description: Provides an interactive REST API and API tokens for integrations.

features.title: Multi-user and enterprise auth
features.description: Supports multiple accounts per instance plus LDAP or reverse-proxy authentication.

pros: Free and open source
Keeps reading data private and self-hosted
Straightforward Goodreads import path for migrating existing libraries
REST API for integrations
LDAP/reverse-proxy auth support for shared or organizational use
cons: Requires self-hosting via JAR or Docker; no official hosted version
Smaller community than Goodreads or The StoryGraph
No official native mobile app

faqs.question: Is Jelu free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source, deployed via a JAR file or Docker on your own server.

faqs.question: Can I import my Goodreads library into Jelu?
faqs.answer: Yes, Jelu supports importing reading history from a Goodreads export or a list of ISBNs.

faqs.question: Does Jelu have an API?
faqs.answer: Yes, it provides an interactive REST API with support for API tokens.

faqs.question: Can multiple people share one Jelu instance?
faqs.answer: Yes, it supports multiple user accounts, including LDAP or reverse-proxy authentication for shared or organizational deployments.

use_cases.title: Private reading tracker
use_cases.description: Individual readers self-host Jelu to track books read and build a private to-read list.

use_cases.title: Shared household or book club library
use_cases.description: A household or book club runs one instance with multiple accounts to track everyone's reading.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Jelu is a free, open-source, self-hosted book tracker with Goodreads import, custom shelves, and a REST API.

enrichment.short_description: Container streaming platform delivering browser-based virtual desktops and app streaming, with a free Community Edition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Kasm Workspaces is a container-streaming platform that delivers virtual desktops, isolated browsers, and applications directly into a user's web browser without any local installation. It's built by Kasm Technologies, founded in 2017 and headquartered in McLean, Virginia.

It's used for browser isolation, remote workspace delivery, and secure access to sensitive or legacy systems, and is offered as a free, self-hosted Community Edition alongside paid personal and team/enterprise tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: IT and security teams needing isolated browsing or remote access, organizations streaming legacy Windows or Linux applications to end users, and teams needing secure access to sensitive or air-gapped systems.

features.title: Browser-based virtual desktops
features.description: Streams full Linux or Windows desktops directly into a web browser without a client install.

features.title: Container-based isolation
features.description: Each session runs in an isolated container, following a zero-trust security model.

features.title: Browser isolation
features.description: Provides disposable, isolated browser sessions to protect against web-based threats.

features.title: Flexible deployment
features.description: Can be deployed on-premises or in the cloud.

features.title: Free Community Edition
features.description: A self-hosted edition available at no cost for individuals, nonprofits, and testing.

features.title: Scalable architecture
features.description: Designed to scale from a single self-hosted node to larger multi-tenant deployments.

pros: Free, self-hosted Community Edition available
Strong container-based isolation and zero-trust security model
No client software needed — runs entirely in the browser
Flexible on-premises or cloud deployment
cons: Paid tiers required for larger teams or enterprise features
Running container-streaming infrastructure at scale adds operational complexity
Primarily aimed at IT/security teams rather than casual consumer use

faqs.question: Is Kasm Workspaces free?
faqs.answer: There's a free, self-hosted Community Edition intended for individuals, nonprofits, and testing, alongside paid personal and team/enterprise plans.

faqs.question: What is Kasm Workspaces used for?
faqs.answer: It's used for browser isolation, streaming remote desktops and applications, and securing access to sensitive or legacy systems.

faqs.question: Can Kasm be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be deployed on your own on-premises infrastructure or in the cloud.

faqs.question: Who makes Kasm Workspaces?
faqs.answer: It's developed by Kasm Technologies, a company founded in 2017 and headquartered in McLean, Virginia.

use_cases.title: Browser isolation
use_cases.description: Security teams give users disposable, isolated browser sessions to reduce exposure to web-based threats.

use_cases.title: Legacy application streaming
use_cases.description: IT departments stream legacy Windows or Linux applications into modern browsers instead of maintaining local installs.

use_cases.title: Secure remote access to sensitive systems
use_cases.description: Organizations use Kasm to provide controlled, isolated access to OT/IoT or otherwise sensitive systems.

features: Intended for individuals, nonprofits, and testing

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Kasm Workspaces streams browser-based virtual desktops and apps via containers, with a free self-hosted Community Edition.
